curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 4 (Derivatives and Risk Management), Learning Module 3: Currency Management: An Introduction

drills.los_text: analyze the effects of currency movements on portfolio risk and return

questions.stem: *Domestic-currency return decomposition* single foreign asset *most likely*:

options.A: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) - 1 where R_FC is foreign-currency return on foreign asset and R_FX is percentage change (appreciation) of foreign currency against domestic currency.
options.B: The domestic-currency return on a single foreign asset is R_DC = R_FC + R_FX, a simple additive decomposition.
options.C: The domestic-currency return equals R_FC × R_FX, the product of the two component returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) - 1 where R_FC is foreign-currency return on foreign asset and R_FX is percentage change (appreciation) of foreign currency against domestic currency; two components are multiplicative not additive.

Correct. Per Equation 1: 'The domestic-currency return on foreign-currency assets can be broken into the foreign-currency asset return and the return on the foreign currency (the percentage appreciation or depreciation of the foreign currency against the domestic currency). These two components of the domestic-currency return are multiplicative.' Illustrated: 'the foreign-currency return on the USD-denominated bond was 10% and the USD appreciated by 5% against the EUR. In this example, the domestic-currency return for the Eurozone investor's holding in the USD-denominated bond was approximately equal to 10% + 5% = 15% (which is close to the exact value of 15.5%).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The simple sum omits the cross-term; the decomposition is multiplicative: (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Multiplying the returns themselves is not the relationship — the GROSS returns compound.

options.A: The multi-asset domestic-currency return is the unweighted average of each asset's foreign-currency return.
options.B: R_DC = Σ ω_i × (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) - 1 where ω_i are portfolio weights (measured in common currency, the domestic currency) that sum to 1.
options.C: Portfolio weights in the R_DC formula are measured in each asset's local currency.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Weights matter, and the currency effect must compound per asset: R_DC = Σ ω_i(1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) − 1.
rationales.B:
Full statement: R_DC = Σ ω_i × (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) - 1 where ω_i are portfolio weights (measured in common currency, the domestic currency) that sum to 1; if short selling is allowed some of ω_i can be less than zero; exchange rate notation must be consistently defined with domestic currency as price currency.

Correct. Per Equation 2: 'R_DC = Σ ω_i (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) − 1 where R_FC,i is the foreign-currency return on the i-th foreign asset, R_FX,i is the appreciation of the i-th foreign currency against the domestic currency, and ω_i are the portfolio weights of the foreign-currency assets (defined as the percentage of the aggregate domestic-currency value of the portfolio) and Σ ω_i = 1. (Note that if short selling is allowed in the portfolio, some of the ω_i can be less than zero.) Again, it is important that the exchange rate notation in this expression (used to calculate R_FX,i) must be consistently defined with the domestic currency as the price currency.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The weights ω_i are measured in a COMMON currency — the investor's domestic currency.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: when R_FC and R_FX are small, cross-term R_FC × R_FX is small so R_DC ≈ R_FC + R_FX.

rationales.A: Additive approximation.
rationales.B: Variance decomposition.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when R_FC and R_FX are small, cross-term R_FC × R_FX is small so R_DC ≈ R_FC + R_FX; then variance approximation σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ(R_FC, R_FX); risk more typically defined in terms of standard deviation because mean and standard deviation measured in same units.

Correct. Per Equations 3-5: 'R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1 = R_FC + R_FX + R_FC × R_FX. When R_FC and R_FX are small, then the cross-term (R_FC × R_FX) is small, and as a result this equation can be approximated as R_DC ≈ R_FC + R_FX... Applying this result to the domestic-currency return approximation... leads to: σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ(R_FC,R_FX).'

questions.stem: *Exchange rate exposure effect on domestic-currency variance* *most likely*:

options.A: because Equation 5 is based on addition of all three terms on right side of equal sign, exchange rate exposure will generally cause variance of domestic-currency returns σ²(R_DC) to increase to more than that of foreign-currency returns σ²(R_FC) considered on their own.
options.B: Exchange rate exposure has no effect on the variance of domestic-currency returns.
options.C: Adding currency exposure always reduces domestic-currency return variance through diversification.

rationales.A:
Full statement: because Equation 5 is based on addition of all three terms on right side of equal sign, exchange rate exposure will generally cause variance of domestic-currency returns σ²(R_DC) to increase to more than that of foreign-currency returns σ²(R_FC) considered on their own; if there was no exchange rate risk, then σ²(R_DC) = σ²(R_FC); using this as base case, adding exchange rate risk exposure to portfolio usually adds to domestic-currency return variance (effect is indeterminate if exchange rate movements are negatively correlated with foreign asset returns).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Note as well that because Equation 5 is based on the addition of all three terms on the right side of the equal sign, exchange rate exposure will generally cause the variance of domestic-currency returns, σ²(R_DC), to increase to more than that of the foreign-currency returns, σ²(R_FC), considered on their own. That is, if there was no exchange rate risk, then it would be the case that σ²(R_DC) = σ²(R_FC). Using this as our base-case scenario, adding exchange rate risk exposure to the portfolio usually adds to domestic-currency return variance (the effect is indeterminate if exchange rate movements are negatively correlated with foreign asset returns).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The variance equation ADDS the FX variance and covariance terms — exposure generally raises total risk.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Reduction requires sufficiently negative correlation; the general case is added variance.

questions.stem: *Example 1 eurozone investor holding Canadian asset* — asset 101→99.80, CAD/EUR 1.45→1.47 *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.B:
R_FC = 99.80/101.00 - 1 = -1.19%; need to convert CAD/EUR quote to EUR as price currency: R_FX = [(1/1.47)/(1/1.45)] - 1 = -1.364%; R_DC = (1 - 1.19%)(1 - 1.364%) - 1 = -2.53%.

Correct. Per Example 1 Solution 1: 'For the eurozone investor, the R_FC = (99.80/101.00) − 1 = −1.19%. Note that, given we are considering the eurozone to be "domestic" for this investor and given the way the R_FX expression is defined, we will need to convert the CAD/EUR exchange rate quote so that the EUR is the price currency. This leads to R_FX = [(1/1.4700)/(1/1.4500)] − 1 = −1.364%. Hence, for the eurozone investor, R_DC = (1 − 1.19%)(1 ‒ 1.364%) − 1 = −2.53%.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 Canadian investor holding eurozone asset* — asset 100.69→101.50, CAD/EUR 1.45→1.47 *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.B: no data.

rationales.C:
R_FC = 101.50/100.69 - 1 = +0.80%; in CAD/EUR quote CAD is price currency so R_FX = 1.4700/1.4500 - 1 = +1.38%; R_DC = (1 + 0.80%)(1 + 1.38%) - 1 = 2.19%.

Correct. Per Example 1 Solution 2: 'For the Canadian investor, the R_FC = (101.50/100.69) − 1 = +0.80%. Given that in the CAD/EUR quote the CAD is the price currency, for this investor the R_FX = (1.4700/1.4500) − 1 = +1.38%. Hence, for the Canadian investor the R_DC = (1 + 0.80%)(1 + 1.38%) − 1 = 2.19%.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 risk calc* Canadian investor σ(R_FC) 3%, σ(R_FX) 2%, ρ +0.5 *most likely*:

options.B: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ = (3%)² + (2%)² + 2(3%)(2%)(0.50) = 9 + 4 + 6 = 19 in %² units = 0.0019; taking square root: σ(R_DC) ≈ 4.36%.

Correct. Per Example 1 Solution 3: 'Because this is a single foreign-currency asset we are considering (not a portfolio of such assets), we can use Equation 5: σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ(R_FC,R_FX). Inserting the relevant data leads to σ²(R_DC) ≈ (3%)² + (2%)² + 2(3%)(2%)(0.50) = 0.0019. Taking the square root of this leads to σ(R_DC) ≈ 4.36%.'

options.A: Portfolio currency risk depends only on the individual variances of each foreign-currency asset.
options.B: domestic-currency risk exposure of overall portfolio σ(R_DC) will depend not only on variances of each of the foreign-currency returns (R_FC) and exchange rate movements (R_FX) but also on how each of these interacts with the others.
options.C: Correlations among currency and asset returns are irrelevant to portfolio construction.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Variances are half the story — the CORRELATIONS among R_FC and R_FX terms complete σ(R_DC).
rationales.B:
Full statement: domestic-currency risk exposure of overall portfolio σ(R_DC) will depend not only on variances of each of the foreign-currency returns (R_FC) and exchange rate movements (R_FX) but also on how each of these interacts with the others; generally speaking, negative correlations among these variables will help reduce overall portfolio's risk through diversification effects; if short-selling is allowed some ω_i can be negative — for two foreign assets with strong positive return correlation, short selling one can create considerable diversification benefits (equivalent to trading movements in price spread).

Correct. Per the reading: 'the domestic-currency risk exposure of the overall portfolio — that is, σ(R_DC) — will depend not only on the variances of each of the foreign-currency returns (R_FC) and exchange rate movements (R_FX) but also on how each of these interacts with the others. Generally speaking, negative correlations among these variables will help reduce the overall portfolio's risk through diversification effects. Note as well that the overall portfolio's risk exposure will depend on the portfolio weights (ω_i) used. If short-selling is allowed in the portfolio, some of these ω_i can be negative as long as the total portfolio weights sum to one. So, for two foreign assets with a strong positive return correlation, short selling one can create considerable diversification benefits for the portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Correlations are central; they determine whether currency exposures offset or compound asset risk.

drills.los_text: discuss strategic choices in currency management

questions.stem: *Two camps of thought on currency management* *most likely*:

options.A: A single unified view governs currency management: all currency risk should always be fully hedged.
options.B: Both schools of thought agree that currency movements are predictable in the short run.
options.C: one camp holds that in the long run currency effects cancel out to zero as exchange rates revert to historical means or fundamental values.

rationales.A: Incorrect. TWO camps exist — and the full-hedge prescription belongs to only one of them.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Short-run predictability is contested; the long-run-mean-reversion camp sees zero expected currency return, while the other sees exploitable opportunity in the interim.
rationales.C:
Full statement: one camp holds that in the long run currency effects cancel out to zero as exchange rates revert to historical means or fundamental values; efficient currency market is zero-sum game — no long-run gains overall to speculating in currencies, especially after netting management/transaction costs — so both currency hedging and actively trading currencies represent a cost to portfolio with little prospect of consistently positive active returns; other camp notes currency movements can have dramatic impact on short-run returns and return volatility and there are pricing inefficiencies — much of currency flow is need-to-do basis, some participants not profit-oriented (central banks, gov agencies) or are 'uninformed traders' (retail); marketplace diversity presents potential for 'harvesting alpha' through active trading.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Concerning beliefs, one camp of thought holds that in the long run currency effects cancel out to zero as exchange rates revert to historical means or their fundamental values. Moreover, an efficient currency market is a zero-sum game (currency "A" cannot appreciate against currency "B" without currency "B" depreciating against currency "A"), so there should not be any long-run gains overall to speculating in currencies, especially after netting out management and transaction costs. Therefore, both currency hedging and actively trading currencies represent a cost to a portfolio with little prospect of consistently positive active returns. At the other extreme, another camp of thought notes that currency movements can have a dramatic impact on short-run returns and return volatility and holds that there are pricing inefficiencies in currency markets.'

questions.stem: *Investment Policy Statement (IPS) role in currency management* *most likely*:

options.A: IPS mandates degree of discretionary currency management allowed in portfolio, how it will be benchmarked, and limits on type of trading policies and tools (e.g., leverage) that can be used.
options.B: The IPS plays no role in currency management, which is left entirely to the trading desk's discretion.
options.C: The IPS specifies daily currency positions rather than the framework governing them.

rationales.A:
Full statement: IPS mandates degree of discretionary currency management allowed in portfolio, how it will be benchmarked, and limits on type of trading policies and tools (e.g., leverage) that can be used; usually specifies general objectives of investment portfolio, risk tolerance and capacity for bearing risk, time horizon, income/liquidity needs, and benchmark; currency risk management policy addresses target proportion of currency exposure to be passively hedged, latitude for active currency management around target, frequency of hedge rebalancing, currency hedge performance benchmark, and hedging tools permitted.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The Investment Policy Statement (IPS) mandates the degree of discretionary currency management that will be allowed in the portfolio, how it will be benchmarked, and the limits on the type of trading polices and tools (e.g., such as leverage) than can be used... Most IPS specify many of the following points: the general objectives of the investment portfolio; the risk tolerance of the portfolio and its capacity for bearing risk; the time horizon over which the portfolio is to be invested; the ongoing income/liquidity needs (if any) of the portfolio; and the benchmark against which the portfolio will measure overall investment returns.' And: 'The currency risk management policy will usually address such issues as the target proportion of currency exposure to be passively hedged; latitude for active currency management around this target; frequency of hedge rebalancing; currency hedge performance benchmark to be used; and hedging tools permitted.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The IPS is the GOVERNING document — mandating permitted discretion, benchmarks, and instrument limits.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Positions are implementation; the IPS sets the policy architecture within which they are taken.

questions.stem: *Two-step process for portfolio optimization with currency* *most likely*:

options.A: Portfolio optimization with currency risk must always be solved in a single simultaneous step.
options.B: many portfolio managers handle asset allocation with currency risk as two-step process: (1) portfolio optimization over fully hedged returns.
options.C: The two-step process optimizes currency exposures first and asset holdings second.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Joint optimization is theoretically cleaner but many managers deliberately separate the problems.
rationales.B:
Full statement: many portfolio managers handle asset allocation with currency risk as two-step process: (1) portfolio optimization over fully hedged returns; (2) selection of active currency exposure, if any; derivative strategies allow various risk exposures in portfolio to be 'unbundled' from each other and managed separately; use of derivatives allows price risk R_FC,i and exchange rate risk R_FX,j of foreign-currency assets to be unbundled and managed separately; starting point is complete currency hedge — portfolio manager will choose exposures to foreign-currency assets first, then decide on appropriate currency exposures afterward.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Confronted with these difficulties, many portfolio managers handle asset allocation with currency risk as a two-step process: (1) portfolio optimization over fully hedged returns; and (2) selection of active currency exposure, if any. Derivative strategies can allow the various risk exposures in a portfolio to be "unbundled" from each other and managed separately. The same applies for currency risks. Because the use of derivatives allows the price risk (R_FC,i) and exchange rate risk (R_FX,j) of foreign-currency assets to be unbundled and managed separately, a starting point for the selection process of portfolio weights would be to assume a complete currency hedge... First the portfolio manager could pick the set of portfolio weights (ω_i) for the foreign-currency assets that optimize the expected foreign-currency asset risk–return trade-off (assuming there is no currency risk). Then the portfolio manager could choose the desired currency exposures for the portfolio and decide whether and by how far to relax the constraint to a full currency hedge for each currency pair.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The order is reversed: (1) optimize the FULLY HEDGED portfolio, then (2) select active currency exposures as an overlay.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: time horizon of IPS important.

rationales.A: Long horizon = less hedging.
rationales.B: PPP mean reversion.
rationales.C:
Full statement: time horizon of IPS important; many practitioners believe that in long run adding unhedged foreign-currency exposure does not affect expected long-run portfolio returns — hence in long run would not matter if portfolio was hedged (indeed portfolio management costs would be reduced without hedging); belief based on view that in long run currencies 'mean revert' to fair value equilibrium level or historical average — expected %ΔS = 0 for sufficiently long time period; view typically draws on expectation that purchasing power parity (PPP) and other international parity conditions will eventually hold; investor with very long investment horizon and few immediate liquidity needs might choose to forgo currency hedging.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The time horizon of the IPS is important. Many investment practitioners believe that in the long run, adding unhedged foreign-currency exposure to a portfolio does not affect expected long-run portfolio returns; hence in the long run, it would not matter if the portfolio was hedged. (Indeed, portfolio management costs would be reduced without a hedging process.) This belief is based on the view that in the long run, currencies "mean revert" to either some fair value equilibrium level or a historical average; that is, that the expected %ΔS = 0 for a sufficiently long time period. This view typically draws on the expectation that purchasing power parity (PPP) and the other international parity conditions that link movements in exchange rates, interest rates, and inflation rates will eventually hold over the long run.'

options.A: often asserted that correlation between foreign-currency returns and foreign-currency asset returns tends to be GREATER for fixed-income portfolios than for equity portfolios.
options.B: Currency-asset correlations are identical for fixed income and equity portfolios, implying the same hedge ratios.
options.C: Equity portfolios should be hedged more heavily than fixed-income portfolios.

rationales.A:
Full statement: often asserted that correlation between foreign-currency returns and foreign-currency asset returns tends to be GREATER for fixed-income portfolios than for equity portfolios; makes intuitive sense: both bonds and currencies react strongly to movements in interest rates whereas equities respond more to expected earnings; implication is that currency exposures provide LITTLE diversification benefit to fixed-income portfolios and that currency risk should be hedged; better argument can be made for carrying currency exposures in global equity portfolios; still more likely to see currency hedging for fixed-income portfolios rather than equity portfolios.

Correct. Per the reading: 'It is often asserted that the correlation between foreign-currency returns and foreign-currency asset returns tends to be greater for fixed-income portfolios than for equity portfolios. This assertion makes intuitive sense: both bonds and currencies react strongly to movements in interest rates, whereas equities respond more to expected earnings. As a result, the implication is that currency exposures provide little diversification benefit to fixed-income portfolios and that the currency risk should be hedged. In contrast, a better argument can be made for carrying currency exposures in global equity portfolios... Nonetheless, it is still more likely to see currency hedging for fixed-income portfolios rather than equity portfolios.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The correlation is asserted to be GREATER for fixed income — currency risk compounds bond risk more directly.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The prescription runs the other way: hedge FIXED INCOME more fully than equities.

options.A: no distinction.
options.B: some currencies — USD, JPY, and CHF in particular — seem to act as safe haven and appreciate in times of market stress.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: USD/JPY/CHF safe haven.
rationales.B:
Full statement: some currencies — USD, JPY, and CHF in particular — seem to act as safe haven and appreciate in times of market stress; keeping some of these currency exposures in portfolio (hedge ratios not set at 100%) can help hedge losses on riskier assets, especially for foreign-currency equity portfolios (which are more risk exposed than bond portfolios); Campbell/Serfaty-de Medeiros/Viceira (2007) found USD tended to appreciate against foreign currencies when global bond prices fell — for non-US investors this would mean under-hedging exposure to USD (hedge ratio less than 100%); for US investors would mean over-hedging their foreign-currency exposures back into USD.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Note that some currencies — the USD, JPY, and CHF in particular — seem to act as a safe haven and appreciate in times of market stress. Keeping some of these currency exposures in the portfolio — having hedge ratios that are not set at 100% — can help hedge losses on riskier assets, especially for foreign currency equity portfolios (which are more risk exposed than bond portfolios).' Also: 'This finding would suggest that keeping some exposure to the US dollar in a global bond portfolio would be beneficial. For non-US investors, this would mean under-hedging the currency exposure to the USD (i.e., a hedge ratio less than 100%), whereas for US investors it would mean over-hedging their foreign-currency exposures back into the USD.'
rationales.C: Safe havens.

options.A: no costs.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: hedging costs come mainly in two forms: trading costs and opportunity costs.

rationales.A: Trading + opportunity costs.
rationales.B: Costs framework.
rationales.C:
Full statement: hedging costs come mainly in two forms: trading costs and opportunity costs; most immediate costs of hedging involve trading expenses in several forms: trading involves dealing on bid-offer spread offered by banks — profit margin based on spreads, more the client trades and 'pays away the spread' more profit is generated by dealer; maintaining 100% hedge and rebalancing frequently with every minor move will be expensive; currency hedges not a 'free good'; optimal hedging decisions balance benefits of hedging against these costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The costs of currency hedging also guide the strategic positioning of the portfolio. Currency hedges are not a "free good" and they come with a variety of expenses that must be borne by the overall portfolio. Optimal hedging decisions will need to balance the benefits of hedging against these costs. Hedging costs come mainly in two forms: trading costs and opportunity costs. The most immediate costs of hedging involve trading expenses, and these come in several forms: Trading involves dealing on the bid–offer spread offered by banks. Their profit margin is based on these spreads, and the more the client trades and "pays away the spread," the more profit is generated by the dealer.'

options.A: there are a variety of approaches to currency management ranging from trying to avoid all currency risk in portfolio to actively seeking foreign exchange risk in order to manage it and enhance portfolio returns.
options.B: Only two currency postures exist: fully hedged or fully unhedged, with nothing in between.
options.C: All institutional investors are required to run identical currency management programs.

rationales.A:
Full statement: there are a variety of approaches to currency management ranging from trying to avoid all currency risk in portfolio to actively seeking foreign exchange risk in order to manage it and enhance portfolio returns; no firm consensus about most effective way to manage currency risk; some investment managers try to hedge all currency risk, some leave portfolios unhedged, others see currency risk as potential source of incremental return and actively trade FX; widely varying management practices reflect variety of factors including investment objectives, investment constraints, and beliefs about currency markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'There are a variety of approaches to currency management, ranging from trying to avoid all currency risk in a portfolio to actively seeking foreign exchange risk in order to manage it and enhance portfolio returns. There is no firm consensus — either among academics or practitioners — about the most effective way to manage currency risk. Some investment managers try to hedge all currency risk, some leave their portfolios unhedged, and others see currency risk as a potential source of incremental return to the portfolio and will actively trade foreign exchange. These widely varying management practices reflect a variety of factors including investment objectives, investment constraints, and beliefs about currency markets.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The range is CONTINUOUS — from full risk avoidance through partial/discretionary hedging to active currency alpha seeking.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The chosen point on the spectrum reflects each investor's objectives, risk tolerance, beliefs, and costs.

drills.los_text: formulate an appropriate currency management program given financial market conditions and portfolio objectives and constraints

questions.stem: *Currency management strategies spectrum* *most likely* range from:

options.A: no range.
options.B: passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Four levels.
rationales.B:
Full statement: passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures; discretionary hedging which allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures; active currency management which seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit; currency overlay programs that aggressively manage currency 'alpha'.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Currency management strategies can be located along a spectrum stretching from: passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures; discretionary hedging, which allows the portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures; active currency management, which seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit; and to currency overlay programs that aggressively manage currency "alpha."'
rationales.C: Four levels.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: passive hedging is rules-based approach to hedging currency exposures completely.

rationales.A: Passive rule-based.
rationales.B: Passive full hedge.
rationales.C:
Full statement: passive hedging is rules-based approach to hedging currency exposures completely; portfolio matches its currency exposures to those of benchmark portfolio; hedging costs and opportunities for gain from active management are set aside in favor of avoiding currency risk; requires periodic rebalancing to maintain hedge as underlying values change.

Correct. Per the reading spectrum: 'passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures' represents one end of the spectrum. This approach relies on rules and complete hedging to eliminate currency risk relative to a benchmark.

options.A: discretionary hedging allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures around a benchmark.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: discretionary hedging allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures around a benchmark; permits manager to leave positions unhedged or partially hedged when doing so is expected to be advantageous based on market view; typical mandate might specify hedge ratio target with allowed range around it (e.g., 100% target with ±10% latitude); manager must operate within IPS constraints.

Correct. Per the reading: 'discretionary hedging, which allows the portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures' is one point along the spectrum. This latitude is bounded by IPS-mandated parameters including 'target proportion of currency exposure to be passively hedged; latitude for active currency management around this target.'
rationales.B: Latitude around benchmark.
rationales.C: Discretionary bounds.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Seek risk for profit.
rationales.B:
Full statement: seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit; requires manager to have market view and derives returns from taking positions consistent with that view; can be based on economic fundamentals, technical analysis, carry trade, or volatility trading; still operates within IPS-mandated risk parameters but has substantial latitude to construct positions distinct from benchmark; goal is to generate positive active return through currency management.

Correct. Per the reading spectrum: 'active currency management, which seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit.' Active currency management requires a market view and can be based on various methodologies (fundamentals, technical analysis, carry trade, volatility trading) as detailed in later LOSes.
rationales.C: Active for profit.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: aggressive management of currency alpha.

rationales.A: Aggressive alpha.
rationales.B: Currency alpha.
rationales.C:
Full statement: aggressive management of currency alpha; entrusts currency exposures to specialized currency manager who is granted wide discretion; goal is to generate currency alpha separately from underlying asset portfolio; overlay manager may take positions outside portfolio's initial currency exposures; distinct from mainstream portfolio management and requires specific expertise.

Correct. Per the reading spectrum: 'currency overlay programs that aggressively manage currency "alpha."' These overlay programs represent the most aggressive end of the spectrum for currency management, treating currency as a distinct source of alpha to be pursued actively.

questions.stem: *IPS variables affecting currency management program design* *most likely*:

options.A: portfolio's currency management program depends on: general objectives of portfolio.
options.B: not relevant.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: portfolio's currency management program depends on: general objectives of portfolio; risk tolerance and capacity for bearing risk; time horizon (longer horizons may support less hedging due to mean reversion); ongoing income/liquidity needs (higher liquidity needs may support more hedging to avoid forced liquidation at bad rates); benchmark against which portfolio measures returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The starting point for organizing the investment plan for any portfolio is the IPS, which is a statement that outlines the broad objectives and constraints of the beneficial owners of the assets. Most IPS specify many of the following points: the general objectives of the investment portfolio; the risk tolerance of the portfolio and its capacity for bearing risk; the time horizon over which the portfolio is to be invested; the ongoing income/liquidity needs (if any) of the portfolio; and the benchmark against which the portfolio will measure overall investment returns.'
rationales.B: IPS drives program.
rationales.C: IPS drivers.

questions.stem: *Effect of market conditions on hedging cost/benefit* *most likely*:

options.A: Market conditions are irrelevant to the cost/benefit calculus behind hedging decisions.
options.B: market conditions affect cost/benefit calculations behind hedging decision — movements in forward points (expected roll yield) or exchange rate volatility (option premiums) affect expected cost of hedge.
options.C: Hedging costs are constant across all market environments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Forward points (expected roll yield) and option volatility pricing move WITH markets, reshaping the calculus.
rationales.B:
Full statement: market conditions affect cost/benefit calculations behind hedging decision — movements in forward points (expected roll yield) or exchange rate volatility (option premiums) affect expected cost of hedge; same hedge structure can be 'rich' or 'cheap' depending on current market conditions; when forward points move against hedger, amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates; opposite occurs when movements in forward points reduce hedging costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Market conditions will affect the cost/benefit calculations behind the hedging decision, as movements in forward points (expected roll yield) or exchange rate volatility (option premiums) affect the expected cost of the hedge; the same hedge structure can be "rich" or "cheap" depending on current market conditions.' Also: 'the tendency to hedge will vary depending on whether implementing the hedge happens to be trading in the same direction of the forward rate bias strategy or against it.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Costs vary systematically — roll yield can favor or punish the hedger as rate differentials shift.

options.A: single answer.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: there is no single 'correct' approach to initiating and managing currency exposures.

rationales.A: No single right answer.
rationales.B: Judgment-based.
rationales.C:
Full statement: there is no single 'correct' approach to initiating and managing currency exposures; different managers likely make different decisions when confronted with same opportunity set; each manager will likely have good reason for their individual decision; most important point is that portfolio manager be aware of all benefits, costs, and risks of chosen strategy and be comfortable that any remaining residual currency risks in hedge are acceptable.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Reflecting all of these considerations, different managers will likely make different decisions when confronted with the same opportunity set; and each manager will likely have a good reason for their individual decision. The most important point is that the portfolio manager be aware of all the benefits, costs, and risks of the chosen strategy and be comfortable that any remaining residual currency risks in the hedge are acceptable.'

drills.los_text: compare active currency trading strategies based on economic fundamentals, technical analysis, carry-trade, and volatility trading

questions.stem: *Economic fundamentals approach* to active currency management *most likely*:

options.A: at heart of approach is assumption that in flexible exchange rate system exchange rates are determined by logical economic relationships that can be modeled.
options.B: The economic fundamentals approach assumes exchange rates are pure random walks unrelated to economic variables.
options.C: The fundamentals approach relies exclusively on chart patterns and past price behavior.

rationales.A:
Full statement: at heart of approach is assumption that in flexible exchange rate system exchange rates are determined by logical economic relationships that can be modeled; simple economic framework based on assumption that in long run real exchange rate will converge to 'fair value' but short- to medium-term factors will shape convergence path; long-run equilibrium level for real exchange rate determined by purchasing power parity or some other model of exchange rate's fair value.

Correct. Per the reading: 'This section sets out a broad framework for developing a view about future exchange rate movements based on underlying fundamentals. In contrast to other methods for developing a market view... at the heart of this approach is the assumption that, in a flexible exchange rate system, exchange rates are determined by logical economic relationships and that these relationships can be modeled. The simple economic framework is based on the assumption that in the long run, the real exchange rate will converge to its "fair value," but short- to medium-term factors will shape the convergence path to this equilibrium... The long-run equilibrium level for the real exchange rate is determined by purchasing power parity or some other model of an exchange rate's fair value.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Random-walk agnosticism is the opposite premise — fundamentals posit LOGICAL ECONOMIC RELATIONSHIPS (e.g., long-run PPP convergence).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Charts belong to technical analysis; fundamentals work from equilibrium values and macro variables.

questions.stem: *Base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if* *most likely*:

options.A: No identifiable factors connect economic conditions to real exchange rate appreciation.
options.B: all else equal, base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is upward movement in: (1) its long-run equilibrium real exchange rate.
options.C: The base currency's real exchange rate appreciates only when its interest rates fall.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framework lists them — equilibrium value, real rate differentials, risk premium compression among the upward drivers.
rationales.B:
Full statement: all else equal, base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is upward movement in: (1) its long-run equilibrium real exchange rate; (2) either its real or nominal interest rates, which should attract foreign capital; (3) expected foreign inflation, which should cause foreign currency to depreciate; (4) the foreign risk premium, which should make foreign assets less attractive compared with base currency nation's domestic assets; real exchange rate should also increase if currently below its long-term equilibrium value.

Correct. Per the reading: 'the base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is an upward movement in its long-run equilibrium real exchange rate; either its real or nominal interest rates, which should attract foreign capital; expected foreign inflation, which should cause the foreign currency to depreciate; and the foreign risk premium, which should make foreign assets less attractive compared with the base currency nation's domestic assets. The real exchange rate should also increase if it is currently below its long-term equilibrium value. All of this makes intuitive sense.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. FALLING rates generally weaken the currency; upward movement in real rate differentials supports appreciation.

options.A: Technical analysis rests on the belief that only future fundamental data matter, making price history irrelevant.
options.B: Technical analysis assumes markets are perfectly efficient, leaving no patterns to exploit.
options.C: (1) market technicians believe that in liquid freely traded market historical price data can be helpful in projecting future price movements — reason: many traders already used useful external data to generate positions so this information is already reflected in current prices.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Price HISTORY is the raw material — technicians hold that past prices inform future prices in liquid markets.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Perfect efficiency would kill the discipline; technicians believe patterns RECUR because human psychology repeats.
rationales.C:
Full statement: (1) market technicians believe that in liquid freely traded market historical price data can be helpful in projecting future price movements — reason: many traders already used useful external data to generate positions so this information is already reflected in current prices; (2) historical patterns in price data have tendency to repeat and this repetition provides profitable trade opportunities — price patterns repeat because market prices reflect human behavior; (3) technical analysis does not attempt to determine where market prices should trade (fair value) but where they will trade — market may eventually converge to fair value in long run but shorter-term trading opportunities available.

Correct. Per the reading: 'technical analysis is based on three broad themes. First, market technicians believe that in a liquid, freely traded market the historical price data can be helpful in projecting future price movements. The reason is because many traders have already used any useful data external to the market to generate their trading positions, so this information is already reflected in current prices... Second, market technicians believe that historical patterns in the price data have a tendency to repeat, and that this repetition provides profitable trade opportunities. These price patterns repeat because market prices reflect human behavior and human [emotions]... Third, technical analysis does not attempt to determine where market prices should trade (fair value, as in fundamental analysis) but where they will trade.'

options.A: price points on dealers' order boards where one would expect to see clustering of bids and offers respectively.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: price points on dealers' order boards where one would expect to see clustering of bids and offers respectively; at these exchange rate levels, price action expected to get 'sticky' because will take more order flow to pierce wall of either bids or offers; but once these price points are breached, price action can be expected to accelerate as stops are triggered (stops = stop-loss orders); often use technical indicators — 200-day moving average often seen as important support/resistance; when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average sometimes seen as price 'break out' point.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Technical analysis also tries to identify what are called support levels and resistance levels, either within ongoing price trends or at their extremities (i.e., turning points). These support and resistance levels are price points on dealers' order boards where one would except to see clustering of bids and offers, respectively. At these exchange rate levels, the price action is expected to get "sticky" because it will take more order flow to pierce the wall of either bids or offers. But once these price points are breached, the price action can be expected to accelerate as stops are triggered... The 200-day moving average of daily exchange rates is often seen as an important indicator of likely support and resistance. Sometimes two moving averages are used to establish when a price trend is building momentum. For example, when the 50-day moving average crosses the 200-day moving average, this is sometimes seen as a price "break out" point.'
rationales.B: Support/resistance.
rationales.C: Chart levels.

options.A: no definition.
options.B: trading strategy of borrowing in low-yield currencies and investing in high-yield currencies.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Borrow low, invest high.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trading strategy of borrowing in low-yield currencies and investing in high-yield currencies; term 'carry' related to cost of carry — that is, carrying or holding an investment; investment has either implicit or explicit cost (borrowing cost) but may also produce income; net cost of carry is difference between these two return rates; based on exploiting well-recognized violation of one of international parity conditions: uncovered interest rate parity.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The carry trade is a trading strategy of borrowing in low-yield currencies and investing in high-yield currencies. The term "carry" is related to what is known as the cost of carry — that is, of carrying or holding an investment. This investment has either an implicit or explicit cost (borrowing cost) but may also produce income. The net cost of carry is the difference between these two return rates. If technical analysis is based on ignoring economic fundamentals, then the carry trade is based on exploiting a well-recognized violation of one of the international parity conditions often used to describe these economic fundamentals: uncovered interest rate parity.'
rationales.C: Carry trade.

options.A: The forward rate bias is the tendency of forward rates to perfectly predict future spot rates.
options.B: The forward rate bias describes central banks' preference for forward intervention over spot intervention.
options.C: persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity described by carry trade is often referred to as forward rate bias.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The bias is the FAILURE of that prediction — the persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity the carry trade exploits.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Intervention style is unrelated; the term names the empirical UIP violation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity described by carry trade is often referred to as forward rate bias; implication of uncovered interest rate parity is that forward rate should be unbiased predictor of future spot rates; historical data show that forward rate is NOT center of distribution for future spot rates — in fact is biased predictor (hence name 'forward rate bias'); with forward rate premium or discount defined as F_P/B - S_P/B, the 'bias' is that premium typically overstates amount of appreciation of base currency and discount overstates amount of depreciation; forward discount or premium often gets even direction of future spot rate movements wrong.

Correct. Per the reading: 'This persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity described by the carry trade is often referred to as the forward rate bias. An implication of uncovered interest rate parity is that the forward rate should be an unbiased predictor of future spot rates. The historical data, however, show that the forward rate is not the center of the distribution for future spot rates; in fact, it is a biased predictor (for example, see Kritzman 1999). Hence the name "forward rate bias." With the forward rate premium or discount defined as F_P/B − S_P/B the "bias" in the forward rate bias is that the premium typically overstates the amount of appreciation of the base currency, and the discount overstates the amount of depreciation. Indeed, the forward discount or premium often gets even the direction of future spot rate movements wrong.'

questions.stem: *Equivalence of carry trade and trading forward rate bias* *most likely*:

options.A: carry trade strategy (borrowing low-yield, investing high-yield) is equivalent to strategy based on trading forward rate bias.
options.B: The carry trade and trading the forward rate bias are unrelated strategies with different return drivers.
options.C: The carry trade profits only when uncovered interest rate parity holds exactly.

rationales.A:
Full statement: carry trade strategy (borrowing low-yield, investing high-yield) is equivalent to strategy based on trading forward rate bias; trading forward rate bias involves buying currencies trading at forward discount and selling currencies trading at forward premium; from covered interest rate parity, being low-yield currency and trading at forward premium is synonymous; being high-yield currency means trading at forward discount; borrowing in low-yield currency (=selling forward premium currency) and investing in high-yield currency (=buying forward discount currency); makes intuitive sense: 'buy low and sell high'.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The carry trade strategy (borrowing in low-yield currencies, investing in high-yield currencies) is equivalent to a strategy based on trading the forward rate bias. Trading the forward rate bias involves buying currencies trading at a forward discount, and selling currencies trading at a forward premium. This makes intuitive sense: It is desirable to buy low and sell high... This equation shows that when the base currency has a lower interest rate than the price currency... the base currency will trade at a forward premium... That is, being low-yield currency and trading at a forward premium is synonymous. Similarly, being a high-yield currency means trading at a forward discount. Borrowing in the low-yield currency and investing in the high-yield currency (the carry trade) is hence equivalent to selling currencies that have a forward premium and buying currencies that have a forward discount (trading the forward rate bias).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. They are EQUIVALENT — borrowing low-yield/investing high-yield mirrors buying forward-discount currencies and selling forward-premium ones.
rationales.C: Incorrect. UIP holding would zero out the trade; the profit comes from UIP's persistent violation.

options.A: no skew.
options.B: occasional large losses mean return distribution for carry trade has pronounced NEGATIVE skew.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Negative skew.
rationales.B:
Full statement: occasional large losses mean return distribution for carry trade has pronounced NEGATIVE skew; negative skew derives from fact that funding currencies (low-yield currencies where borrowing occurs) are typically safe haven currencies such as EUR, CHF, and JPY; investment currencies (high-yielding currencies) are typically currencies perceived to be higher risk, such as several emerging market currencies; any time global financial markets under stress there is flight to safety causing rapid movements in exchange rates and usually panicked unwinding of carry trades; carry trade sometimes characterized as 'picking up nickels in front of a steamroller'.

Correct. Per the reading: 'These occasional large losses mean that the return distribution for the carry trade has a pronounced negative skew. This negative skew derives from the fact that the funding currencies of the carry trade (the low-yield currencies in which borrowing occurs) are typically the safe haven currencies, such as the EUR, CHF, and JPY. In contrast, the investment currencies (the high-yielding currencies) are typically currencies perceived to be higher risk, such as several emerging market currencies. Any time global financial markets are under stress there is a flight to safety that causes rapid movements in exchange rates, and usually a panicked unwinding of carry trades... Because of the tendency for long periods of relatively small gains in the carry trade to be followed by brief periods of large losses, the carry trade is sometimes characterized as "picking up nickels in front of a steamroller."'
rationales.C: Negative skew.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: type of active trading style unique to option markets.

rationales.A: Vol trading.
rationales.B: Vol trading defn.
rationales.C:
Full statement: type of active trading style unique to option markets; use of options allows trader through variety of trading strategies to unbundle and isolate all various risk factors (the Greeks) and trade them separately; once initial option position taken, trader has exposure to all Greeks; unwanted risk exposures can be hedged away, leaving only desired risk exposure to express specific directional view; volatility trading focuses on vega — sensitivity of option premium to small change in implied volatility (indication of volatility risk); trader expresses view on future movements in implied volatility, not the direction of underlying.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Another type of active trading style is unique to option markets and is known as volatility trading (or simply "vol trading")... The most important concept to grasp in terms of volatility trading is that the use of options allows the trader, through a variety of trading strategies, to unbundle and isolate all of the various risk factors (the Greeks) and trade them separately. Once an initial option position is taken (either long or short), the trader has exposure to all of the various Greeks/risk factors. The unwanted risk exposures, however, can then be hedged away, leaving only the desired risk exposure to express that specific directional view... the trader is expressing a view on the future movements in implied volatility, or in other words, is engaged in volatility trading... By engaging in volatility trading, the trader is expressing a view about the future volatility of exchange rates but not their direction.'

options.A: long straddle = long ATM put + long ATM call (both same strike).
options.B: same structure.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: long straddle = long ATM put + long ATM call (both same strike); deltas -0.5 and +0.5 so net delta of position is zero (delta neutral); profitable in more volatile markets when either put or call go sufficiently in the money to cover upfront cost of two option premiums; long strangle = long OTM put + long OTM call (same expiry, same degree of being out of money); because OTM options being bought, cost is cheaper — but conversely does not pay off until spot rate passes OTM strike levels; risk-reward for strangle more moderate than straddle.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One simple option strategy that implements a volatility trade is a straddle, which is a combination of both an at-the-money (ATM) put and an ATM call. A long straddle buys both of these options. Because their deltas are −0.5 and +0.5, respectively... the net delta of the position is zero; that is, the long straddle is delta neutral. This position is profitable in more volatile markets, when either the put or the call go sufficiently in the money to cover the upfront cost of the two option premiums paid... A similar option structure is a strangle position for which a long position is buying out-of-the-money (OTM) puts and calls with the same expiry date and the same degree of being out of the money... Because OTM options are being bought, the cost of the position is cheaper — but conversely, it also does not pay off until the spot rate passes the OTM strike levels. As a result, the risk–reward for a strangle is more moderate than that for a straddle.'
rationales.B: Straddle vs strangle.
rationales.C: Two positions.

drills.los_text: describe how changes in factors underlying active trading strategies affect tactical trading decisions

questions.stem: *Effect of yield differential change on carry trade* *most likely*:

options.A: no effect.
options.B: changes in yield differential between currencies alter attractiveness of carry trade.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Yield spread drives carry.
rationales.B:
Full statement: changes in yield differential between currencies alter attractiveness of carry trade; if high-yield currency's rate increases while low-yield currency's rate remains stable, yield advantage of high-yield currency widens making carry trade more attractive; long periods of yield differential persistence between high-yield and low-yield currencies encourage build-up of carry trade positions; positive returns can remain in place long enough to present attractive investment opportunities; guide to riskiness is volatility of outright forward (not spot) rate movements — lower volatility is better for carry trade position.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The gains that one can earn through the carry trade... can be seen as the risk premiums earned for carrying an unhedged position... Long periods of market stability can make these extra returns enticing to many investors, and the longer the yield differential persists between high-yield and low-yield currencies, the more carry trade positions will have a tendency to build up.' And: 'One guide to the riskiness of the carry trade is the volatility of outright forward (not spot) rate movements for the currency pair; all else equal, lower volatility is better for a carry trade position.'
rationales.C: Yield gap matters.

questions.stem: *Effect of expected monetary policy change on currency view* *most likely*:

options.A: An expected but unpriced monetary tightening has no implication for the currency's near-term direction.
options.B: A tightening that is not fully priced into markets would be expected to depreciate the tightening country's currency.
options.C: if authorities are expected to tighten monetary policy and change not fully priced into market, currency of tightening country would be expected to appreciate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Unpriced policy shifts are precisely what move currencies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The direction is APPRECIATION — higher expected rates draw capital toward the tightening currency.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if authorities are expected to tighten monetary policy and change not fully priced into market, currency of tightening country would be expected to appreciate; increase in real or nominal interest rates should attract foreign capital, supporting base currency; surprise rate hike would cause currency to appreciate against most currencies; tactical trading decisions should reflect market view of forthcoming rate action — traders may position in options that profit from expected direction of movement.

Correct. Per the reading (Example 3 setup): 'Turning her attention to the economic situation in India, McYelland believes that the Indian authorities are about to tighten monetary policy, and that this change has not been fully priced into the market. She reconsiders her short-term view for the Indian rupee (i.e., the INR/USD spot rate) after conducting this analysis.' And per the fundamentals discussion: 'the base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is an upward movement in... either its real or nominal interest rates, which should attract foreign capital.'

questions.stem: *Effect of higher expected inflation differential* *most likely*:

options.A: if country's expected inflation rises relative to another country's, its currency should depreciate.
options.B: no effect.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if country's expected inflation rises relative to another country's, its currency should depreciate; because base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if expected foreign inflation rises (which should cause foreign currency to depreciate); if analyst expects inflation to accelerate in New Zealand while Japan's remains stable, JPY/NZD should decrease (NZD depreciate against JPY); market view of relative inflation is key input for fundamentals-based currency views.

Correct. Per the reading: 'the base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is an upward movement in... expected foreign inflation, which should cause the foreign currency to depreciate.' Also per Example 3 setup where an expectation of accelerating New Zealand inflation relative to stable Japan inflation would suggest NZD depreciation against JPY.
rationales.B: Higher inflation = depreciation.
rationales.C: Inflation up = FX down.

options.A: A trader expecting higher exchange rate volatility with no directional view should sell straddles to collect premium.
options.B: if trader expects exchange rate volatility to increase without directional view, appropriate response is to buy volatility (e.g., long straddle or long strangle).
options.C: Volatility expectations cannot be expressed through option positions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Selling volatility PROFITS from calm — the long-vol view calls for BUYING it, classically via a long straddle.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if trader expects exchange rate volatility to increase without directional view, appropriate response is to buy volatility (e.g., long straddle or long strangle); if volatility expected to increase and CLP/USD expected to become much more volatile but no strong view on direction, buying options provides positive exposure to vega; option premiums increase with implied volatility, so if trader can position before increase in implied volatility, can benefit from repricing.

Correct. Per Example 3 setup: 'she believes that the exchange rate between Chilean peso and the US dollar (CLP/USD) is about to become much more volatile than usual, although she has no strong views about whether the CLP will appreciate or depreciate.' The tactical response would be to enter a delta-neutral long volatility position (straddle or strangle). Per the reading: 'This position is profitable in more volatile markets, when either the put or the call go sufficiently in the money to cover the upfront cost of the two option premiums paid.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Options are the canonical vehicle; delta-neutral structures isolate the volatility view.

options.A: no signals.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average, sometimes seen as price 'break out' point.

rationales.A: Moving avg signals.
rationales.B: Chart-based signal.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average, sometimes seen as price 'break out' point; if current spot rate is close to 200-day moving average, that level acts as key support/resistance; technicians would time position entry/exit based on chart patterns and moving average signals; when JPY/NZD price at 77.1905 and 200-day moving average at 77.5035, chart shows spot rate below moving average — moving average acts as resistance to further upward move.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The 200-day moving average of daily exchange rates is often seen as an important indicator of likely support and resistance. Sometimes two moving averages are used to establish when a price trend is building momentum. For example, when the 50-day moving average crosses the 200-day moving average, this is sometimes seen as a price "break out" point.' Example 3 illustrates: 'One of McYelland's colleagues, Catalina Ortega, is a market technician... Ortega notices that the 200-day moving average is at 77.5035 and the current spot rate is 77.1905.'

questions.stem: *Best carry trade fund/invest choice among yield differentials* *most likely*:

options.A: look for widest yield differential to maximize carry.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: look for widest yield differential to maximize carry; from Example 3 data (Switzerland -0.80%, US 0.30%, Poland 1.26%, Mexico 5.98%): funding in CHF (lowest yield -0.80%) and investing in MXN (highest yield 5.98%) gives largest yield differential of 6.78%; alternative funding in USD investing in MXN gives smaller 5.68%; funding CHF investing PLN gives only 2.06%; largest positive spread offers best carry potential (subject to volatility considerations).

Correct. Per Example 3: yield levels are Switzerland -0.80%, US 0.30%, Poland 1.26%, Mexico 5.98%. The best carry trade among the choices is 'B. CHF and invest in MXN' because it maximizes the yield differential (6.78% spread), assuming volatility considerations are similar. This is greater than USD/MXN (5.68% spread) or CHF/PLN (2.06% spread).
rationales.B: CHF fund, MXN invest.
rationales.C: Fund CHF/invest MXN.

questions.stem: *Directional view impact on option choice for hedging* *most likely*:

options.A: A trader with a strong directional view should prefer deep-ITM options for maximum leverage.
options.B: when trader has directional view expecting significant move, deep-OTM options provide leveraged exposure — larger percentage change in premium for given change in spot.
options.C: Option moneyness is irrelevant when expressing directional currency views.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Deep-ITM options behave like the underlying with little leverage; deep-OTM options deliver the largest PERCENTAGE payoffs if the move materializes.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when trader has directional view expecting significant move, deep-OTM options provide leveraged exposure — larger percentage change in premium for given change in spot; low-delta option (5-delta or 10-delta) is 'like highly leveraged lottery ticket on event occurring'; with surprise event odds swing in favor; if surprise rate hike expected to cause NZD appreciation, put option on NZD/AUD (which would depreciate as NZD is base currency and appreciates) would benefit — 5-delta put option has more extreme price reaction on premiums than higher-delta option.

Correct. Per Example 3 solution: 'A 5-delta option is deep-OTM, but the price reaction on the option premiums will be more extreme than a higher-delta option. That is to say, the percentage change in the premiums for a 5-delta option for a given percentage change in the spot exchange rate will be higher than the percentage change in premiums for a 25-delta option. In a sense, a very low delta option is like a highly leveraged lottery ticket on the event occurring. With a surprise rate hike, the odds would swing in Bhattathiri's favor.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Moneyness sets the risk-reward: cheap OTM strikes provide leveraged exposure to significant expected moves.

questions.stem: *Convergence of long-run vs short-run factors* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: exchange rate forecast is mix of long-term, medium-term, and short-term factors.

rationales.A: Multi-horizon.
rationales.B: Long/short mix.
rationales.C:
Full statement: exchange rate forecast is mix of long-term, medium-term, and short-term factors; long-run equilibrium real exchange rate is anchor for exchange rates and point of long-run convergence for exchange rate movements; movements in short- to medium-term factors (nominal interest rates, expected inflation) affect timing and path of convergence to long-run equilibrium; can be very demanding to model how each of these separate effects change over time and affect exchange rates.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In summary, the exchange rate forecast is a mix of long-term, medium-term, and short-term factors. The long-run equilibrium real exchange rate is the anchor for exchange rates and the point of long-run convergence for exchange rate movements. Movements in the short- to medium-term factors (nominal interest rates, expected inflation) affect the timing and path of convergence to this long-run equilibrium.'

drills.los_text: describe how forward contracts and FX (foreign exchange) swaps are used to adjust hedge ratios

options.A: simultaneous spot and forward transaction — one leg is buying the base currency and the other is selling it.
options.B: no definition.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: simultaneous spot and forward transaction — one leg is buying the base currency and the other is selling it; FX swaps are used to renew outstanding forward contracts once they mature, to 'roll them forward'; rolling forward hedging position involves selling base currency at then-current spot exchange rate to settle current forward contract, and then going long another far-dated forward contract.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An FX swap is a simultaneous spot and forward transaction; one leg of the swap is buying the base currency and the other is selling it. FX swaps are used to renew outstanding forward contracts once they mature, to "roll them forward."' Also: 'this rolling forward will involve selling the base currency at the then-current spot exchange rate to settle the forward contract, and then going long another far-dated forward contract (i.e., an FX swap transaction).'
rationales.B: FX swap = spot + fwd.
rationales.C: Spot + forward.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: magnitude of roll yield is |(F_P/B - S_P/B)/S_P/B|.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Roll yield sign rules.
rationales.B:
Full statement: magnitude of roll yield is |(F_P/B - S_P/B)/S_P/B|; sign depends on whether investor needs to buy or sell base currency forward to maintain hedge; positive roll yield results from buying base currency at forward discount OR selling it at forward premium (intuition: profitable to 'buy low and sell high'); otherwise roll yield is negative (positive cost).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The magnitude of roll yield is given by |(F_P/B ‒ S_P/B)/S_P/B| where "||" indicates absolute value. The sign depends on whether the investor needs to buy or to sell the base currency forward in order to maintain the hedge. A positive roll yield results from buying the base currency at a forward discount or selling it at a forward premium (the intuition here is that it is profitable to "buy low and sell high"). Otherwise, the roll yield is negative (i.e., a positive cost).'
rationales.C: Roll yield.

questions.stem: *Buying base currency at forward premium hedge outcome* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: if hedge requires buying base currency at forward premium: pay forward price of A.

rationales.A: Negative roll.
rationales.B: Buy high sell low.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if hedge requires buying base currency at forward premium: pay forward price of A; as time passes price of forward contract 'rolls down the curve' toward Price B as contract's settlement date approaches; at settlement roll forward hedge by selling base currency at then-current spot rate (Price B, lower than A) and going long another far-dated forward contract; buying high (Price A) and selling low (Price B) will be cost to portfolio — roll yield negative; the larger the gap between A and B at contract initiation, the less likely to be positive.

Correct. Per the reading (Exhibit 7 discussion): 'assume that to implement the hedge requires buying the base currency in the P/B quote, and that the base currency is trading at a forward premium (as shown in Exhibit 7). By using a long position in a forward contract to implement this hedge, it means paying the forward price of A. All else equal, as time passes the price of the forward contract will "roll down the curve" toward Price B as the forward contract's settlement date approaches... Note that the portfolio manager originally bought the base currency at Price A and then subsequently sold it at a lower Price B — and that buying high and selling low will be a cost to the portfolio. Or put differently, all else equal, the roll yield would be negative in this case.'

questions.stem: *Equivalence carry trade / trade forward rate bias / earn positive roll yield* *most likely*:

options.A: carry trade (borrow low-yield, invest high-yield) is equivalent to trading forward rate bias (buy forward discount currency, sell forward premium currency) and to earning positive roll yield.
options.B: The carry trade, forward rate bias trading, and earning roll yield are three unrelated sources of currency return.
options.C: Positive roll yield accrues to hedgers who buy forward-premium currencies.

rationales.A:
Full statement: carry trade (borrow low-yield, invest high-yield) is equivalent to trading forward rate bias (buy forward discount currency, sell forward premium currency) and to earning positive roll yield; negative roll yield typically indicates hedger was trading against forward rate bias — buying currency at forward premium or selling at forward discount; hedger with negative roll yield is also essentially entering into negative carry trade, in effect borrowing at high rates and investing at low rates; on average, this will not be a winning strategy.

Correct. Per the reading (Exhibit 8): 'The Carry Trade and Roll Yield: Implementing the carry trade | High-yield currency | Low-yield currency. Trading the forward rate bias | Forward discount currency | Forward premium currency. Earning a positive roll yield.' Also: 'a negative roll yield typically indicates that the hedger was trading against the forward rate bias by buying a currency at a forward premium (as in Exhibit 7) or selling a currency at a forward discount. This is the exact opposite of trading the forward rate bias, which is to buy at a discount and sell at a premium. Given the equivalence between the forward rate bias and the carry trade, by trading against the forward rate bias the hedger with a negative roll yield is also essentially entering into negative carry trade, in effect borrowing at high rates and investing at low rates. On average, this will not be a winning strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The three are ONE strategy in different clothing — borrow low/invest high ≡ buy discount/sell premium ≡ earn positive roll yield.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Buying premium currencies forward LOCKS IN negative roll; the positive roll accrues on the discount side.

questions.stem: *Hedge ratio adjustment based on cost/benefit* *most likely*:

options.A: Hedge ratios are held constant regardless of movements in forward points.
options.B: when forward points move against hedger amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates.
options.C: Deteriorating hedge economics lead managers to increase hedging activity to lock in rates before further decline.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The cost/benefit ratio drives behavior — hedge ratios respond to forward point movements.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when forward points move against hedger amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates; opposite occurs when movements in forward points reduce hedging costs; tendency to hedge will vary depending on whether implementing hedge trades in same direction as forward rate bias strategy or against it; if setting up hedge involves selling low-yield currency and buying high-yield currency (implicit carry position), more likely portfolio will be fully hedged or over-hedged; trading against forward rate bias likely to lead to lower hedge ratios.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Because the level of and movements in forward points can either enhance or reduce currency-hedged returns, it explains an observed tendency in foreign exchange markets for the amount of currency hedging to generally vary with movements in forward points. As forward points move against the hedger, the amount of hedging activity typically declines as the cost/benefit ratio of the currency hedge deteriorates. The opposite occurs when movements in forward points reduce hedging costs.' Also: 'if setting up the hedge involves selling the low-yield currency and buying the high-yield currency in the P/B pair (i.e., an implicit carry position), then the more likely the portfolio will be fully hedged or even over-hedged. The opposite is also true: Trading against the forward rate bias is likely to lead to lower hedge ratios, all else equal.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The response runs the other way: as forward points move AGAINST the hedger, hedging activity typically DECLINES.

questions.stem: *Kwun Tong GBP hedge decision (Example 4)* — HKD/GBP spot 10.60, 6-mo forward 10.5895, 6-mo forecast 10.20 *most likely*:

options.A: Kwun Tong should hedge the GBP exposure because the 0.099% forward discount represents the full cost of hedging over the period.
options.B: The example provides no basis for comparing the forward discount to the expected GBP depreciation.
options.C: long GBP against HKD; HKD/GBP selling at small forward discount of 0.099% vs current spot — this is expected roll yield, NOT in firm's favor because to implement hedge Kwun Tong would sell GBP (base currency) at price lower than current spot.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The small discount is the expected ROLL YIELD, not the full hedge cost — the decision weighs it against the forecast move to 10.20.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The data are complete: spot 10.60, forward 10.5895, six-month forecast 10.20 — the forecast depreciation dwarfs the 0.099% roll.
rationales.C:
Full statement: long GBP against HKD; HKD/GBP selling at small forward discount of 0.099% vs current spot — this is expected roll yield, NOT in firm's favor because to implement hedge Kwun Tong would sell GBP (base currency) at price lower than current spot; but market strategist expects GBP to depreciate by 3.77% against HKD; negative carry of hedging is small in comparison to potential loss; both considerations argue for hedging this exposure.

Correct. Per Example 4 Solution 1: 'Kwun Tong is long the GBP against the HKD, and HKD/GBP is selling at a small forward discount of 0.099% compared with the current spot rate. All else equal, this is the expected roll yield — which is not in the firm's favor, in this case, because to implement the hedge Kwun Tong would be selling GBP, the base currency in the quote, at a price lower than the current spot rate. However, the firm's market strategist expects the GBP to depreciate by 3.77% against the HKD. Thus, the negative carry of hedging is small in comparison to the potential loss. Both of these considerations argue for hedging this exposure.'

questions.stem: *Kwun Tong ZAR hedge decision (Example 4)* — HKD/ZAR spot 0.5210, 6-mo forward 0.5082, 6-mo forecast 0.5100 *most likely*:

options.A: long ZAR against HKD; HKD/ZAR selling at forward discount of -2.5% vs current spot.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: long ZAR against HKD; HKD/ZAR selling at forward discount of -2.5% vs current spot; would require selling ZAR (base currency) at lower price than spot; roll yield goes against firm — expected cost of hedge = 2.5%; strategist forecasts ZAR depreciation against HKD of 2.11%; risk-neutral investor would NOT hedge because expected cost of hedge (2.5%) more than expected depreciation (2.11%); but point forecast comes with uncertainty; decision depends on trade-offs between (1) level of risk aversion of firm and (2) conviction firm held in currency forecast.

Correct. Per Example 4 Solution 2: 'Kwun Tong is long the ZAR against the HKD, and HKD/ZAR is selling at a forward discount of −2.5% compared with the current spot rate.
