Implementing the hedge would require the firm to sell the base currency in the quote, the ZAR, at a price lower than the current spot rate. This would imply that, all else equal, the roll yield would go against the firm; that is, the expected cost of the hedge would be 2.5%. But the firm's strategist also forecasts that the ZAR will depreciate against the HKD by 2.11%. This makes the decision to hedge less certain. A risk-neutral investor would not hedge because the expected cost of the hedge is more than the expected depreciation of the ZAR. But this is only a point forecast and comes with a degree of uncertainty — there is a risk that the HKD/ZAR spot rate might depreciate by more than the 2.5% cost of the hedge. In this case, the decision to hedge the currency risk would depend on the trade-offs between (1) the level of risk aversion of the firm; and (2) the conviction the firm held in the currency forecast — that is, the level of certainty that the ZAR would not depreciate by more than 2.5%.'
rationales.B: Trade-off decision.
rationales.C: Risk aversion.

options.A: Setting the hedge ratio requires only minimizing expected hedging costs.
options.B: when setting hedge ratio, portfolio manager must balance effect of expected future exchange rate movements on portfolio returns against expected effect of roll yield (expected cost of hedge).
options.C: The hedge ratio decision ignores the investor's risk tolerance entirely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Cost is one side; the EFFECT of expected currency moves on returns and risk completes the balance.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when setting hedge ratio, portfolio manager must balance effect of expected future exchange rate movements on portfolio returns against expected effect of roll yield (expected cost of hedge); risk-neutral manager would not hedge if net expected value of hedge is negative; risk-averse manager might still implement hedge regardless of negative net expected value because it is possible market forecast is wrong and actual depreciation may be higher than cost of hedge; risk-averse manager must weigh certainty of hedge cost against risk that actual unhedged currency losses might be higher.

Correct. Per the reading: 'when setting the hedge ratio, the portfolio manager must balance the effect of expected future exchange rate movements on portfolio returns against the expected effect of the roll yield (i.e., the expected cost of the hedge)... A risk-neutral manager would not hedge under these circumstances because the net expected value of the hedge is negative. But a risk-averse manager might still implement the hedge regardless of the negative net expected value. The reason is because it is possible that the market forecast is wrong and that the actual depreciation of the base currency (and realized loss to the portfolio) may be higher than the 6% cost of the hedge. The risk-averse manager must then weigh the certainty of a hedge that costs 6% against the risk that actual unhedged currency losses might be much higher than that.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Risk tolerance anchors the trade-off between expected value and return volatility.

drills.los_text: describe trading strategies used to reduce hedging costs and modify the risk–return characteristics of a foreign-currency portfolio

options.A: A protective put pairs a short position in the underlying currency with a long put option.
options.B: A protective put is a written put option held against an uncovered currency exposure.
options.C: long position in underlying with put option — matching long position in underlying with put option is known as protective put strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The underlying position is LONG; the put protects its downside.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The put is PURCHASED, not written — long spot plus long put defines the structure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: long position in underlying with put option — matching long position in underlying with put option is known as protective put strategy; if manager is long base currency and needs to sell to implement hedge, buy ATM put option on P/B currency pair; if P/B rate goes down (P appreciates, B depreciates) by expiry, manager can exercise option, implement hedge, and guarantee selling price at strike; if P/B rate increases (P depreciates, B appreciates), manager can simply let option expire and collect currency gains; option premium is upfront cost.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Consider the case of a portfolio manager who is long the base currency in the P/B quote and needs to sell this currency to implement the hedge. One approach is to simply buy an at-the-money put option on the P/B currency pair. Matching a long position in the underlying with a put option is known as a protective put strategy. Suppose the current spot rate is 1.3650 and the strike price on the put option bought is 1.3650. If the P/B rate subsequently goes down (P appreciates and B depreciates) by the expiry date, the manager can exercise the option, implement the hedge, and guarantee a selling price of 1.3650. But if the P/B rate increases (P depreciates and B appreciates), the manager can simply let the option expire and collect the currency gains.'

options.A: way to reduce cost of using options is to accept some downside risk by using OTM option, such as 25- or 10-delta option.
options.B: no benefit.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: way to reduce cost of using options is to accept some downside risk by using OTM option, such as 25- or 10-delta option; these options will be less costly but also do NOT fully protect portfolio from adverse currency movements; makes sense to insure against larger risks but accept some smaller day-to-day price movements in currencies; analogous to home/car insurance with deductible: cheaper insurance policy accepting responsibility for minor events but insuring against extreme damage.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One way to reduce the cost of using options is to accept some downside risk by using an OTM option, such as a 25- or 10-delta option. These options will be less costly, but also do not fully protect the portfolio from adverse currency movements. Conversely, it makes sense to insure against larger risks but accept some smaller day-to-day price movements in currencies. As an analogy, it may be possible to buy a home or car insurance policy with a zero deductible — but the premiums would be exorbitant. It may be more rational to have a cheaper insurance policy and accept responsibility for minor events, but insure against extreme damage.'
rationales.B: OTM put cheaper.
rationales.C: OTM cheaper.

options.A: A currency collar buys an ATM call and sells an ATM put to create symmetric exposure.
options.B: one strategy to obtain downside protection at lower cost than straight protective put: buy OTM put option and write OTM call option — selling some upside potential (writing call) using option premiums to help pay cost of long put.
options.C: The risk reversal structure requires paying more premium than a straight protective put.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The collar's legs sit OUT of the money — buy the OTM put, write the OTM call around the exposure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: one strategy to obtain downside protection at lower cost than straight protective put: buy OTM put option and write OTM call option — selling some upside potential (writing call) using option premiums to help pay cost of long put; similar to creating collar in fixed-income markets; portfolio protected against downside movements but upside limited to strike price on OTM call option — exchange rate risk confined to corridor or 'collar'; in professional FX markets, long position in call option + short position in put option is called risk reversal; buying 25-delta call and writing 25-delta put = long position in 25-delta risk reversal; the collar structure (buying put, writing call) is SHORT position in risk reversal.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One strategy to obtain downside protection at a lower cost than a straight protective put position is to buy an OTM put option and write an OTM call option. Essentially, the portfolio manager is selling some of the upside potential for movements in the base currency (writing a call) and using the option's premiums to help pay the cost of the long put option being purchased. This approach is similar to creating a collar in fixed-income markets. The portfolio is protected against downside movements, but its upside is limited to the strike price on the OTM call option; the exchange rate risk is confined to a corridor or "collar." In professional FX markets, having a long position in a call option and a short position in a put option is called a risk reversal. For example, buying a 25-delta call and writing a 25-delta put is referred to as a long position in a 25-delta risk reversal. The position used to create the collar position we just described (buying a put, writing a call) would be a short position in a risk reversal.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cost REDUCTION is the point — the written call's premium offsets the put's, cheaper than the outright put.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: variation of short risk reversal position.

rationales.A: Put spread structure.
rationales.B: Put spread.
rationales.C:
Full statement: variation of short risk reversal position; used to reduce upfront cost of buying protective put; buy put option and write another put option to help cover cost of long put's premiums; typically structured by buying OTM put, and writing deeper-OTM put; both options same maturity; example: current spot 1.3550, buy put with strike 1.3500 and write put with strike 1.3450 — no hedge protection between 1.3550 and 1.3500; portfolio hedged from 1.3500 down to 1.3450; at spot rates below 1.3450 portfolio becomes unhedged again; reduces cost of hedge but at cost of more limited downside protection.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A variation of the short risk reversal position is a put spread, which is also used to reduce the upfront cost of buying a protective put. The short risk reversal is structured by buying a put option and writing a call option: the premiums received by writing the call help cover the cost of the put. Similarly, the put spread position involves buying a put option and writing another put option to help cover the cost of the long put's premiums. This position is typically structured by buying an OTM put, and writing a deeper-OTM put to gain income from premiums; both options involved have the same maturity. To continue our previous example, with the current spot rate at 1.3550, the portfolio manager might set up the following put spread: buy a put with a strike of 1.3500 and write a put with a strike of 1.3450. The payoff on the put spread position will then be as follows: there is no hedge protection between 1.3550 and 1.3500; the portfolio is hedged from 1.3500 down to 1.3450; at spot rates below 1.3450, the portfolio becomes unhedged again.'

options.A: to make put spread cheaper or zero-cost, portfolio manager could write larger notional amount for deeper-OTM option — e.g., ratio for notionals for options written vs bought might be 1:2.
options.B: A 1x2 put spread buys two puts at the higher strike and writes one put at the lower strike.
options.C: The 1x2 structure always increases the net premium paid versus a standard put spread.

rationales.A:
Full statement: to make put spread cheaper or zero-cost, portfolio manager could write larger notional amount for deeper-OTM option — e.g., ratio for notionals for options written vs bought might be 1:2; in standard FX market notation this would be 1 × 2 put spread (option with exercise price closest to being ATM given first); may now be approximately zero-cost but not without risks: for spot rates below deep-OTM strike portfolio has now seen exposure to base currency double-up (because of 1:2 proportion of notionals) and at worse spot rate; creating zero-cost structure with 1 × 2 put spread is equivalent to adding LEVERAGE — selling more options than buying; requires careful management.

Correct. Per the reading: 'the portfolio manager could write a larger notional amount for the deeper-OTM option; for example, the ratio for the notionals for the options written versus bought might be 1:2. (In standard FX market notation, this would be a 1 × 2 put spread — the option with exercise price closest to being ATM is given first...) Although this structure may now be (approximately) zero-cost it is not without risks: for spot rates below 1.3450 the portfolio has now seen its exposure to the base currency double-up (because of the 1:2 proportion of notionals) and at a worse spot exchange rate for the portfolio on top of it. Creating a zero-cost structure with a 1 × 2 put spread is equivalent to adding leverage to the options position, because you are selling more options than you are buying. This means that this put spread position will have to be carefully managed.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The RATIO runs the other way — one put bought, TWO deeper-OTM puts written.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The doubled short leg's premium REDUCES net cost, potentially to zero — accepting exposure below the lower strike.

options.A: no structure.
options.B: extension of risk reversal and put spread.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Three-legged.
rationales.B:
Full statement: extension of risk reversal and put spread; core of hedge is long position in put option; to reduce cost, short risk reversal writes call option while put spread writes deep-OTM put option; manager can do both: long protective put and write both call and deep-OTM put; option structure sometimes referred to as seagull spread; 'seagull' indicates option structure with at least three individual options, options at most distant strikes (wings) on opposite side of market from middle strike(s) (body); if current spot 1.3550: long ATM put at 1.3550 (body), short OTM put at 1.3500, short OTM call at 1.3600 (wings) = short seagull; provides full downside protection from 1.3550 to 1.3500 and upside participation to 1.3600.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An alternative, and somewhat safer approach, would be to combine the original put spread position (1:1 proportion of notionals) with a covered call position. This is simply an extension of the concept behind risk reversals and put spreads... the manager can always do both: that is, be long a protective put and then write both a call and a deep-OTM put. This option structure is sometimes referred to as a seagull spread. As with the names for other option strategies based on winged creatures, the "seagull" indicates an option structure with at least three individual options, and in which the options at the most distant strikes — the wings — are on the opposite side of the market from the middle strike(s) — the body. For example, if the current spot price is 1.3550, a seagull could be constructed by going long an ATM put at 1.3550 (the middle strike is the "body"), short an OTM put at 1.3500, and short an OTM call at 1.3600 (the latter two options are the "wings"). Because the options in the "wings" are being written (sold) this is called a short seagull position. The risk/return profile of this structure gives full downside protection from 1.3550 to 1.3500 (at which point the short put position neutralizes the hedge) and participation in the upside potential in spot rate movements to 1.3600.'
rationales.C: Seagull.

options.A: A knock-in option is a standard vanilla option that ceases to exist when the spot rate touches a trigger level.
options.B: Knock-in and knock-out features have no effect on an option's premium.
options.C: knock-in option = vanilla option that only comes into existence when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified level (trigger level, called 'barrier', not same as strike price).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Extinction-on-touch is the KNOCK-OUT; the knock-in only COMES INTO existence at the trigger.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The barrier features cheapen the option — conditional existence is worth less than unconditional.
rationales.C:
Full statement: knock-in option = vanilla option that only comes into existence when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified level (trigger level, called 'barrier', not same as strike price); knock-out option = vanilla option that ceases to exist when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified barrier level; because these options only exist under certain circumstances, they are more restrictive than vanilla options and hence are CHEAPER; but knock-in/out features provide less upside potential and/or downside protection.

Correct. Per the reading: 'when the spot exchange rate touches a pre-specified level (this trigger level, called the "barrier," is not the same as the strike price). Similarly a knock-out option is a vanilla option that ceases to exist when the spot exchange rate touches some pre-specified barrier level. Because these options only exist (i.e., get knocked-in or knocked-out) under certain circumstances, they are more restrictive than vanilla options and hence are cheaper. But again, the knock-in/out features provide less upside potential and/or downside protection.'

options.A: also called binary options or all-or-nothing options.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: also called binary options or all-or-nothing options; expiry value of in-the-money vanilla option varies based on amount of difference between expiry level and strike price; in contrast, digital options pay out a FIXED amount if they are determined to be in-the-money; American digital options pay fixed amount if they 'touch' their exercise level at any time before expiry (even if by single pip); characteristic of 'extreme payoff' options makes them almost akin to lottery ticket; because of these large payoffs, digital options usually cost MORE than vanilla options with same strike price; digitals provide highly leveraged exposure to movements in spot rate — more appropriate as trading tools for active currency management rather than as hedging tools.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Digital options are also called binary options, or all-or-nothing options. The expiry value of an in-the-money vanilla option varies based on the amount of difference between the expiry level and strike price. In contrast, digital options pay out a fixed amount if they are determined to be in-the-money. For example, American digital options pay a fixed amount if they "touch" their exercise level at any time before expiry (even if by a single pip). This characteristic of "extreme payoff" options makes them almost akin to a lottery ticket. Because of these large payoffs, digital options usually cost more than vanilla options with the same strike price. But digitals also provide highly leveraged exposure to movements in the spot rate. This makes these exotic products more appropriate as trading tools for active currency management, rather than as hedging tools.'
rationales.B: Binary all-or-nothing.
rationales.C: Digital options.

options.A: No methods exist for reducing the cost of a core currency hedge.
options.B: for manager with long exposure to currency, cost of 'core' hedge (short position in forward or upfront premium on long put) can be reduced by: (a) writing options to gain upfront premiums.
options.C: Cost mitigation always requires abandoning downside protection entirely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A menu exists — written options against the core hedge, spreads, ratio structures, exotic features.
rationales.B:
Full statement: for manager with long exposure to currency, cost of 'core' hedge (short position in forward or upfront premium on long put) can be reduced by: (a) writing options to gain upfront premiums; (b) varying strike prices of options written or bought; (c) varying notional amounts of derivative contracts; keep in mind lower hedging costs equate to less downside protection and/or upside potential; higher allowed discretion for active management, lower risk aversion, and firmer market view lead to hedge structured to allow risk exposures — positioning in derivatives that 'lean the same way' as market view.

Correct. Per the reading summary: '3. The cost of the hedge is focused on its "core." For a manager with a long exposure to a currency, the cost of this "core" hedge will be the implicit costs of a short position in a forward contract (no upside potential, possible negative roll yield) or the upfront premium on a long position in a put option. Either of these two forms of insurance can be expensive. However, there are various cost mitigation methods that can be used alone or in combination to reduce these core hedging costs: a. Writing options to gain upfront premiums. b. Varying the strike prices of the options written or bought. c. Varying the notional amounts of the derivative contracts.' Also: 'The higher the allowed discretion for active management, the lower the risk aversion; and the firmer a particular market view is held, the more the hedge is likely to be structured to allow risk exposures in the portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Protection is RESHAPED rather than abandoned — each cost reduction trades away some protection or upside, deliberately.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: second determinant of option's premium is time value, heavily influenced by volatility in exchange rates.

rationales.A: Theta works against long.
rationales.B: Theta cost.
rationales.C:
Full statement: second determinant of option's premium is time value, heavily influenced by volatility in exchange rates; regardless of exchange rate volatility, options are always moving toward expiry; in general, time value of option is always declining — time decay of option's value (theta, one of Greeks) similar in concept to negative roll yield on forward contracts; time decay always works against owner of an option; means long option positions have known ongoing cost even in stable markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The second determinant of an option's premium is its time value, which in turn is heavily influenced by the volatility in exchange rates. Regardless of exchange rate volatility, however, options are always moving toward expiry. In general, the time value of the option is always declining. This is the time decay of the option's value (theta, one of the "Greeks" of option prices, describes this effect) and is similar in concept to that of negative roll yield on forward contracts described earlier. Time decay always works against the owner of an option.'

drills.los_text: describe the use of cross-hedges, macro-hedges, and minimum-variance-hedge ratios in portfolios exposed to multiple foreign currencies

options.A: hedging one currency exposure using derivative contract in different but correlated currency.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: hedging one currency exposure using derivative contract in different but correlated currency; useful when direct hedge is unavailable or too expensive; e.g., can use long-short positions in positively correlated currencies to achieve much lower portfolio risk through benefits of cross hedging; requires appropriate correlation between currency being hedged and hedging instrument; some types of cross hedges often referred to as macro hedges.

Correct. Per the reading example: 'having long and short positions in positively correlated currencies can lead to much lower portfolio risk, through the benefits of cross hedging.' Cross hedging involves using a different but correlated exposure to hedge the target exposure. 'Some types of cross hedges are often referred to as macro hedges.'
rationales.B: Correlated proxy.
rationales.C: Cross hedge.

options.A: A macro hedge targets a single asset's currency exposure with its own dedicated forward contract.
options.B: cross hedge focused on entire portfolio rather than individual assets or currency pairs, particularly when individual asset price movements are highly correlated.
options.C: Macro hedging is unrelated to cross hedging techniques.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Single-asset targeting is the DIRECT hedge; the macro hedge addresses the PORTFOLIO-level exposure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: cross hedge focused on entire portfolio rather than individual assets or currency pairs, particularly when individual asset price movements are highly correlated; another way of viewing macro hedge: seeing portfolio not just as collection of financial assets but as collection of risk exposures — various risk exposures typically defined in categories such as term risk, credit risk, liquidity risk; can also be defined in terms of financial scenarios portfolio is exposed to (recession, financial sector stress, inflation); often macro hedges defined in terms of financial scenario they protect portfolio from; e.g., putting gold in portfolio, using volatility overlay program, using derivative based on index rather than specific assets or currencies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some types of cross hedges are often referred to as macro hedges. The reason is because the hedge is more focused on the entire portfolio, particularly when individual asset price movements are highly correlated, rather than on individual assets or currency pairs. Another way of viewing a macro hedge is to see the portfolio not just as a collection of financial assets, but as a collection of risk exposures. These various risk exposures are typically defined in categories, such as term risk, credit risk, and liquidity risk. These risks can also be defined in terms of the potential financial scenarios the portfolio is exposed to, such as recession, financial sector stress, or inflation. Often macro hedges are defined in terms of the financial scenario they are designed to protect the portfolio from. Putting gold in the portfolio sometimes serves this purpose... Using a volatility overlay program can also hedge the portfolio... Using a derivative product based on an index, rather than specific assets or currencies, can also define a macro hedge.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The macro hedge IS a cross hedge — focused on the whole portfolio, often via instruments (e.g., baskets) not matched to any single position.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: mathematical approach to determining optimal cross hedging ratio using OLS regression.

rationales.A: OLS-based ratio.
rationales.B: Min-var hedge.
rationales.C:
Full statement: mathematical approach to determining optimal cross hedging ratio using OLS regression; regression y_t = α + β x_t + ε_t used to minimize variance of ε; y = percentage change in value of asset to be hedged, x = percentage change in value of hedging instrument (both in domestic currency terms); minimum-variance hedge ratio (β̂) = correlation(y,x) × [std.dev(y) / std.dev(x)] = covariance(y,x)/variance(x); calculated coefficient gives optimal hedging ratio minimizing tracking error.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A mathematical approach to determining the optimal cross hedging ratio is known as the minimum-variance hedge ratio. Recall that regression analysis based on ordinary least squares (OLS) is used to minimize the variance of ε̂, the residual between actual and fitted values of the regression y_t = α + β x_t + ε_t... This same principle can be used to minimize the tracking error between the value of the hedged asset and the hedging instrument. In the regression formula, we substitute the percentage change in the value of the asset to be hedged for y_t, and the percentage change in value of the hedging instrument for x_t... The calculated coefficient in this regression (β̂) gives the optimal hedging ratio... It can be shown that the formula for the minimum-variance hedge ratio... is mathematically equal to: covariance(y,x)/variance(x) = correlation(y,x) × [std.dev(y)/std.dev(x)].'

questions.stem: *Kilmarnock SMI minimum-variance hedge example* — β̂ = 1.35 *most likely*:

options.A: 10 years monthly data on CHF/GBP spot and SMI.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 10 years monthly data on CHF/GBP spot and SMI; McYelland inverts CHF/GBP to GBP/CHF so domestic currency is price; regresses R_DC = α + β(%ΔS_GBP/CHF) + ε; result β̂ = 1.35 = minimum-variance hedge ratio; makes sense because β̂ represents sensitivity of domestic-currency return on portfolio to percentage changes in spot rate; SMI seems very sensitive to appreciation of CHF; correlation +0.6 between %ΔSMI and %ΔS_GBP/CHF; recommends minimum-variance hedge ratio set at approximately 135% — long CHF1M SMI exposure hedged with short CHF/GBP position of CHF1.35M.

Correct. Per the reading: 'RDC = α + β(%ΔS_GBP/CHF) + ε. The calculated regression coefficients show that α̂ = −0.21 and β̂ = 1.35. McYelland interprets these results to mean that the estimated β̂-coefficient is the minimum-variance hedge ratio. This conclusion makes sense because β̂ represents the sensitivity of the domestic-currency return on the portfolio to percentage changes in the spot rate. In this case, the return on the SMI seems very sensitive to the appreciation of the CHF. Indeed, over the 10 years of data she collected, McYelland notices that the correlation between %ΔSMI and %ΔS_GBP/CHF is equal to +0.6. On the basis of these calculations, she recommends that the minimum-variance hedge ratio for Kilmarnock Capital's exposure to the SMI be set at approximately 135%. This recommendation means that a long CHF1,000,000 exposure to the SMI should be hedged with a short position in CHF against the GBP of approximately CHF1,350,000.'
rationales.B: 135% hedge.

options.A: The minimum-variance hedge ratio applies to all hedges, including direct forward hedges of a currency against itself.
options.B: calculating minimum-variance hedge ratio typically applies only for 'indirect' hedges based on cross hedging or macro hedges.
options.C: Direct hedges require regression-based hedge ratios estimated from historical data.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A direct hedge needs no estimation — the forward matches the exposure one-for-one.
rationales.B:
Full statement: calculating minimum-variance hedge ratio typically applies only for 'indirect' hedges based on cross hedging or macro hedges; NOT typically applied to 'direct' hedge in which exposure to spot rate is hedged with forward contract in that same currency pair; correlation between movements in spot rate and its forward contract likely very close to +1; variance in spot price movements and movements in forward contract price also approximately equal; therefore β̂ regression estimate very close to 1 and minimum-variance hedge ratio close to 100% — undertaking regression is superfluous.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Calculating the minimum-variance hedge ratio typically applies only for "indirect" hedges based on cross hedging or macro hedges; it is not typically applied to a "direct" hedge in which exposure to a spot rate is hedged with a forward contract in that same currency pair. This is because the correlation between movements in the spot rate and its forward contract is likely to be very close to +1. Likewise, the variance in spot price movements and movements in the price of the forward contract are also likely to be approximately equal. Therefore, calculating the minimum-variance hedge ratio by regressing changes in the spot exchange rate against changes in the forward rate will almost always result is a β̂ regression estimate very close to 1, and hence a minimum-variance hedge ratio close to 100%. So, undertaking the regression analysis is superfluous.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Regression-based MVHR belongs to INDIRECT hedges (cross hedges, macro hedges) where the instrument imperfectly tracks the exposure.

options.A: Replacing a direct hedge with a cross hedge or macro hedge introduces no additional risk.
options.B: Basis risk arises only in exchange-traded futures, never in OTC hedging structures.
options.C: any time direct currency hedge (spot rate hedged against own forward contract) is replaced with indirect hedge (cross hedge, macro hedge), basis risk is brought into portfolio.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The substitution CREATES basis risk — the hedge instrument and the exposure can decouple.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Basis risk follows from imperfect tracking, whatever the venue; any indirect hedge carries it.
rationales.C:
Full statement: any time direct currency hedge (spot rate hedged against own forward contract) is replaced with indirect hedge (cross hedge, macro hedge), basis risk is brought into portfolio; reflects fact that price movements in exposure being hedged and price movements in cross hedge instrument are not perfectly correlated; correlation will change with time and sometimes both dramatically and unexpectedly; for minimum-variance hedge ratio, risk expressed as instability in β̂ coefficient estimate as more data become available.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The portfolio manager must be aware that any time a direct currency hedge (i.e., a spot rate hedged against its own forward contract) is replaced with an indirect hedge (cross hedge, macro hedge), basis risk is brought into the portfolio. This risk reflects the fact that the price movements in the exposure being hedged and the price movements in the cross hedge instrument are not perfectly correlated, and that the correlation will change with time — and sometimes both dramatically and unexpectedly. For a minimum-variance hedge ratio, this risk is expressed as instability in the β̂ coefficient estimate as more data become available.'

questions.stem: *Currency basket derivatives* for macro hedge *most likely*:

options.A: one macro hedge specific to foreign exchange markets uses derivatives based on fixed-weight baskets of currencies (available in both exchange-traded and OTC form).
options.B: Currency basket derivatives are unavailable in foreign exchange markets.
options.C: Basket derivatives hedge each currency pair individually with separate contracts.

rationales.A:
Full statement: one macro hedge specific to foreign exchange markets uses derivatives based on fixed-weight baskets of currencies (available in both exchange-traded and OTC form); in multi-currency portfolio may not always be cost efficient to hedge each single currency separately — macro hedge using currency basket derivatives is alternative approach; can protect portfolio against broad currency movements without needing to individually hedge each currency exposure.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One macro hedge specific to foreign exchange markets uses derivatives based on fixed-weight baskets of currencies (such derivatives are available in both exchange-traded and OTC form). In a multi-currency portfolio, it may not always be cost efficient to hedge each single currency separately, and in these situations a macro hedge using currency basket derivatives is an alternative approach.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Fixed-weight currency basket products exist in both OTC and exchange-traded form.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The basket's point is AGGREGATION — one instrument against the portfolio's blended currency exposure, accepting basis risk for cost efficiency.

questions.stem: *Kilmarnock S&P 500 MVHR calc* — σ(%ΔS_GBP/USD) 2.7%, σ(R_DC) 4.4%, ρ 0.2, $10M investment *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A: Long $3.3M USD/GBP fwd.
rationales.B:
Full statement: h = ρ × [σ(R_DC)/σ(R_FX)] = 0.2 × (4.4%/2.7%) = 0.33 or 33%; for USD10M investment in S&P 500 (long position), Kilmarnock Capital would want to be short approximately USD3.3M in forward contract; because standard market quote for this currency pair is USD/GBP, to be short USD means one has to buy GBP → long position in USD/GBP forward contract of notional size USD3.3 million.

Correct. Per the reading solution: 'The formula for the minimum-variance hedge ratio (h) is: h = ρ(R_DC; R_FX) × [σ(R_DC)/σ(R_FX)]. After inputting the data from the table, this equation solves to 0.33. This means that for a USD10 million investment in the S&P 500 (long position), Kilmarnock Capital would want to be short approximately USD3.3 million in a forward contract. Because the standard market quote for this currency pair is USD/GBP, to be short the USD means one would have to buy the GBP; that is, a long position in a USD/GBP forward contract.'

drills.los_text: discuss challenges for managing emerging market currency exposures

questions.stem: *Two most important challenges for EM currency management* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: (1) higher trading costs than the major currencies under 'normal' market conditions.

rationales.A: Cost + tail risk.
rationales.B: Cost + illiquidity.
rationales.C:
Full statement: (1) higher trading costs than the major currencies under 'normal' market conditions; (2) increased likelihood of extreme market events and severe illiquidity under stressed market conditions; many emerging market currencies are thinly traded causing higher transaction costs (bid-offer spreads); may also be fewer derivatives products to choose from especially exchange-traded products.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Managing emerging market currency exposure involves unique challenges. Perhaps the two most important considerations are (1) higher trading costs than the major currencies under "normal" market conditions, and (2) the increased likelihood of extreme market events and severe illiquidity under stressed market conditions. Many emerging market currencies are thinly traded, causing higher transaction costs (bid–offer spreads). There may also be fewer derivatives products to choose from, especially exchange-traded products.'

options.A: for EM cross rates like CLP/THB, typically no direct trade because insufficient trade or capital flows between countries.
options.B: Emerging market cross rates like CLP/THB trade in deep, direct interbank markets.
options.C: Cross-rate trades through an intermediary currency eliminate all transaction costs.

rationales.A:
Full statement: for EM cross rates like CLP/THB, typically no direct trade because insufficient trade or capital flows between countries; any trade between these two currencies would go through major intermediary currency (usually USD); trade broken into two legs: trade in CLP/USD pair and another in THB/USD pair; may go through different traders/desks at same bank or one leg at one bank and other at different bank; time zone issues affecting liquidity — one leg may be relatively liquid while other more thinly traded; liquidity in most EM currencies typically deepest in their domestic time zones; two bid-offer spreads to cover.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, there is no reason why an investor in Chile (which uses the Chilean peso, currency code CLP) could not have an investment in assets denominated in the Thai baht (THB). But the CLP/THB cross is likely to be very thinly traded; there simply are not enough trade or capital flows between these two countries. Typically, any trade between these two currencies would go through a major intermediary currency, usually the USD. Hence, the trade would be broken into two legs: a trade in the CLP/USD pair and another in the THB/USD pair. These trades might go through different traders or trading desks at the same bank; or perhaps one leg of the trade would be done at one bank and the other leg through a different bank. There may also be time zone issues affecting liquidity... liquidity in most emerging market currencies is typically deepest in their domestic time zones. In any event, there are two bid–offer spreads — one for each leg of the trade — to be covered.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Direct markets are typically ABSENT — trade and capital flows between such pairs are too thin.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The two-leg trade through USD (or EUR) DOUBLES the spreads paid; the intermediary route is a necessity, not a cost saving.

questions.stem: *Fat tails and negative skew in EM* *most likely*:

options.A: Emerging market currency returns are well described by the normal distribution.
options.B: investment return probability distributions for currency (and other) trades subject to such relatively frequent extreme events have FATTER TAILS than normal distribution as well as pronounced NEGATIVE SKEW.
options.C: Extreme events in EM currencies are too rare to affect risk management practice.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The distributions display FAT TAILS and NEGATIVE SKEW — normality understates the crash risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: investment return probability distributions for currency (and other) trades subject to such relatively frequent extreme events have FATTER TAILS than normal distribution as well as pronounced NEGATIVE SKEW; risk measurement and control tools (such as value at risk, or VaR) that depend on normal distributions can be misleading and greatly understate risks; many investment performance measures also based on normal distribution — Sharpe ratio can look very attractive during times of relative tranquility but this seeming outperformance can disappear into deep losses faster than most investors can react.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The investment return probability distributions for currency (and other) trades subject to such relatively frequent extreme events have fatter tails than the normal distribution as well as a pronounced negative skew. Risk measurement and control tools (such as value at risk, or VaR) that depend on normal distributions can be misleading under these circumstances and greatly understate the risks the portfolio is actually exposed to. Many investment performance measures are also based on the normal distribution. Historical investment performance measured by such indexes as the Sharpe ratio can look very attractive during times of relative tranquility; but this seeming outperformance can disappear into deep losses faster than most investors can react.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The extreme events are relatively FREQUENT — periodic devaluations and regime breaks shape the risk profile.

questions.stem: *Forward hedging cost in EM currency crisis* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: hedging long exposure to foreign currency typically involves selling foreign currency forward.

rationales.A: Crisis raises hedge cost.
rationales.B: Deep discount.
rationales.C:
Full statement: hedging long exposure to foreign currency typically involves selling foreign currency forward; when currencies under severe downward pressure, central banks often react by hiking policy rate to support domestic currency; but higher interest rates in country → all else equal, deeper forward discount for its currency (enforced by covered interest rate parity arbitrage); having to sell currency forward at increasingly deep discounts will cause losses through negative roll yield and undermine cost effectiveness of hedging program.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The occurrence of currency crises can also affect hedging strategies based on forward contracts. Recall that hedging a long exposure to a foreign currency typically involves selling the foreign currency forward. However, when currencies are under severe downward pressure, central banks often react by hiking the policy rate to support the domestic currency. But recall that the higher interest rates go in a country, then, all else equal, the deeper the forward discount for its currency (enforced by the arbitrage conditions of covered interest rate parity). Having to sell the currency forward at increasingly deep discounts will cause losses through negative roll yield and undermine the cost effectiveness of the hedging program.'

options.A: currencies of many emerging market countries trade with some form of capital controls.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: currencies of many emerging market countries trade with some form of capital controls; where capital controls exist and delivery in controlled currency is limited by local government, often possible to use NDFs; similar to regular forward contracts but cash settled (in non-controlled currency of currency pair) rather than physically settled; non-controlled currency for NDFs usually USD or some other major currency; important currencies with NDFs include Chinese yuan (CNY), Korean won (KRW), Russian ruble (RUB), Indian rupee (INR), Brazilian real (BRL); NDF is essentially cash-settled 'bet' on movement in spot rate of these currencies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Currencies of many emerging market countries trade with some form of capital controls. Where capital controls exist and delivery in the controlled currency is limited by the local government, it is often possible to use what are known as non-deliverable forwards (NDFs). These are similar to regular forward contracts, but they are cash settled (in the non-controlled currency of the currency pair) rather than physically settled (the controlled currency is neither delivered nor received). The non-controlled currency for NDFs is usually the USD or some other major currency. A partial list of some of the most important currencies with NDFs would include the Chinese yuan (CNY), Korean won (KRW), Russian ruble (RUB), Indian rupee (INR), and Brazilian real (BRL). The NDF is essentially a cash-settled "bet" on the movement in the spot rate of these currencies.'
rationales.B: NDF definition.
rationales.C: NDF.

questions.stem: *NDF credit risk vs vanilla forward* *most likely*:

options.A: An NDF carries the same credit risk as an outright deliverable forward of equal notional.
options.B: credit risk of NDF typically LOWER than for outright forward because principal sums in NDF do not move, unlike with outright 'vanilla' forward contract.
options.C: NDFs carry higher credit risk than deliverable forwards because they settle in a controlled currency.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The NDF's credit risk is LOWER — only the net settlement amount is at risk.
rationales.B:
Full statement: credit risk of NDF typically LOWER than for outright forward because principal sums in NDF do not move, unlike with outright 'vanilla' forward contract; in the BRL/USD NDF illustration cash pay-off was small (USD2,169.98) — there was no delivery of USD1M against receipt of BRL5,518,000; but NDFs exist because of some form of government involvement in FX markets — sudden changes in government policy can lead to sharp movements in spot and NDF rates, often reversing investment gains earned during long periods of seeming market calm; implicit market risk of NDF embodies element of 'tail risk'.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The credit risk of an NDF is typically lower than for the outright forward because the principal sums in the NDF do not move, unlike with an outright "vanilla" forward contract. For example, in the illustration the cash pay-off to the "bet" was the relatively small amount of USD2,169.98 — there was no delivery of USD1,000,000 against receipt of BR5,518,000. Conversely, as noted previously, NDFs exist because of some form of government involvement in foreign exchange markets. Sudden changes in government policy can lead to sharp movements in spot and NDF rates, often reversing any investment gains earned during long periods of seeming (but artificial) market calm. The implicit market risk of the NDF embodies an element of "tail risk."'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Settlement is in the FREE currency (typically USD) and covers only the rate difference — principal never moves.

options.A: NDF pricing always matches covered interest rate parity exactly, as with deliverable forwards.
options.B: NDF pricing is unrelated to onshore interest rates or capital flow conditions.
options.C: when capital controls exist, free cross-border flow of capital that enforces arbitrage condition underlying covered interest rate parity no longer functions consistently.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Capital controls BREAK the arbitrage that enforces CIRP — NDF pricing can diverge and take on its own dynamics.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Onshore conditions matter, but the controls sever the arbitrage link, letting the NDF price reflect offshore supply and demand.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when capital controls exist, free cross-border flow of capital that enforces arbitrage condition underlying covered interest rate parity no longer functions consistently; therefore pricing on NDFs need not be exactly in accord with covered interest rate parity theorem; NDF pricing will reflect individual supply and demand conditions (and risk premia) in offshore market, which need not be same as onshore market of specific emerging market country; some most active participants in NDF market are offshore hedge funds and proprietary traders making directional bets rather than corporate/institutional portfolio managers hedging; volatility in net speculative demand for EM exposure can affect level of forward points; type and strictness of capital controls can vary among EMs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'when capital controls exist, the free cross-border flow of capital that enforces the arbitrage condition underlying covered interest rate parity no longer functions consistently. Therefore, the pricing on NDFs need not be exactly in accord with the covered interest rate parity theorem. Instead, NDF pricing will reflect the individual supply and demand conditions (and risk premia) in the offshore market, which need not be the same as the onshore market of the specific emerging market country. Some of the most active participants in the NDF market are offshore hedge funds and proprietary traders making directional bets on the emerging market currency, rather than corporate or institutional portfolio managers hedging currency exposures. Volatility in the net speculative demand for emerging market exposure can affect the level of forward points. We also note that the type and strictness of capital controls can vary among emerging markets; hence, the need for knowledge of local market regulations is another factor influencing currency risk management in these markets.'
